data.title: Six charts to understand Coingecko's Q1 crypto industry report
data.description:
Source: Coingecko; Compiled by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

In the first quarter of 2026, with the downward trend at the end of 2025 compounded by global geopolitical turmoil, the cryptocurrency market transitioned from a significant correction to a sustained "crypto winter." The total market capitalization of the crypto market fell by 20.4%, wiping out $622 billion, closing at $2.4 trillion at the end of the first quarter of 2026, a drop of approximately 45% from the high point in October 2025. In mid-January, Kevin Warsh was nominated as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, signaling a possible shift in US monetary policy towards a hawkish stance, further accelerating the market contraction.

Despite the overall market weakness, this quarter saw a clear flow of safe-haven funds, and the dominant positions of exchanges and public blockchains underwent significant changes. This "Crypto Industry Report Q1 2026" covers the overall crypto market landscape, Bitcoin and Ethereum analysis, in-depth analysis of the decentralized finance (DeFi) ecosystem, and a performance review of centralized exchanges (CEXs) and decentralized exchanges (DEXs). CoinGecko's Q1 2026 Crypto Industry Report: Five Key Highlights

  1. The total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies fell by 20.4% in Q1 2026, closing at $2.4 trillion at the end of March.

  2. The total market capitalization of stablecoins remained flat at $309.9 billion, with USDT supply experiencing its first decline since Q2 2022.

  3. Crude oil led the market in Q1 2026, with a gain of 76.9%; Bitcoin fell 22.0% in tandem with the stock market.

  4. Centralized exchange spot trading volume fell by 39.1% to $2.7 trillion in Q1 2026, after hitting a high of $0.8 trillion in March.

  5. Solana continues to dominate decentralized exchange spot trading volume, reaching a 30.6% market share in Q1 2026. Driven by 24/7 demand for crude oil trading, commodity perpetual contracts currently account for approximately 30% of Hyperliquid's open interest. The total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies fell by 20.4% in Q1 2026, closing at $2.4 trillion at the end of March. The total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies fell 20.4% (a loss of $622 billion) in the first quarter of 2026, to $2.4 trillion. This decline follows a sustained downtrend that began in late 2025, marking two consecutive quarters of decline. The decline was primarily concentrated in mid-January to early February, coinciding with Kevin Warsh's nomination as the next Federal Reserve Chairman. Despite the subsequent US-Iran conflict in the first quarter, the market remained range-bound. The current total market capitalization is approximately 45% lower than its October 2025 high. Daily trading activity also declined significantly, with average daily trading volume falling to $117.8 billion, a 27.2% decrease compared to the first quarter. 2. The total market capitalization of stablecoins remained flat at $309.9 billion, while USDT supply saw its first decline since Q2 2022. In Q1 2026, the total market capitalization of stablecoins increased slightly by $1.6 billion (0.5%), reaching $309.9 billion at the end of the quarter. Despite the overall market pullback, the stablecoin sector remained stable, highlighting its role as a pillar of liquidity. Tether's USDT supply saw its first significant decline since Q2 2022, falling 1.6% (a reduction of $3 billion), with a market capitalization of $184.1 billion at the end of Q1, representing a 59% market share. Meanwhile, Circle's USDC grew 2.4% (an increase of $1.8 billion), reaching $77.1 billion. Sky's USDS and WLFI's USD1 both achieved double-digit growth: USDS surged 30.8% (an increase of $2.8 billion) with the launch of Sky Agents; while USD1 grew 32.5% (an increase of $1.1 billion) after the Binance WLFI airdrop. Following significant deleveraging at the end of 2025, Ethena's USDe contraction slowed significantly, falling only 6.6% in Q1 (a reduction of $400 million), with a market capitalization of $5.9 billion at the end of the quarter.

    3. Crude oil led the market in the first quarter of 2026, with a surge of 76.9%; Bitcoin fell 22.0% in tandem with the stock market

    Crude oil performed exceptionally well in the first quarter of 2026, surging by 76.9%, reversing the decline of 2025. This surge was mainly driven by the global supply shock triggered by the US-Iran conflict.

    Crude oil performed exceptionally well in the first quarter of 2026, surging by 76.9%, reversing the decline of 2025. This surge was mainly driven by the global supply shock triggered by the US-Iran conflict.

    ... Gold (+8.1%) saw its upward momentum cool slightly, but its record-breaking upward trend since 2025 continues, primarily benefiting from continued central bank buying and its status as a core safe-haven asset amidst significant geopolitical turmoil this quarter. Bitcoin (BTC) fell 22.0%, continuing to underperform all major asset classes. US stock indices also weakened during the same period, with the Nasdaq falling 7.1% and the S&P 500 falling 4.8%, both marking their worst quarterly returns since 2022. Meanwhile, the US dollar index (DXY) rose slightly by 1.4%, driven by safe-haven inflows triggered by the Iran war.

    4. In the first quarter of 2026, the total trading volume of CEX spot trading platforms fell by 39.1% to $2.7 trillion, with March hitting a monthly low of $0.8 trillion

    In the first quarter of 2026, the total trading volume of the top ten spot centralized exchanges (CEXs) reached $2.7 trillion, a 39.1% decrease compared to $4.5 trillion in the fourth quarter of 2025.

    Trading volume remained above $1 trillion in January, but continued to decline for the remainder of the first quarter. March saw the weakest performance, recording only $0.8 trillion in trading volume, the lowest level since November 2023. Binance continued to lead, holding a 37.0% market share in the first quarter. MEXC was another exchange with a double-digit market share, accounting for 10.0%. Trading volume at all of the top ten centralized spot exchanges declined in the first quarter, with drops ranging from -23% to -55%. HTX experienced the largest drop, with quarterly trading volume falling from $294.4 billion in the fourth quarter of 2025 to $133.6 billion in the first quarter of 2026, and its market share declining to 4.9%, ranking tenth. 5. Solana continues to dominate DEX spot trading volume, with a market share of 30.6% in Q1 2026. Despite a 26.5% decrease in trading volume, Solana still holds the top spot in public blockchain spot trading volume in Q1 2026 with a quarterly share of 30.6%. However, in March, it was surpassed by Ethereum: Ethereum's share was 27%, while Solana's was 26%. Binance Smart Chain (BSC) slightly surpassed Ethereum's 23.7% share in Q1, maintaining its second-place ranking. However, due to a larger drop in trading volume for BSC than for Ethereum, it is likely to fall to third place in Q2. Monad launched its mainnet in November 2025 at the beginning of the bear market, and its trading volume has gradually increased since then. It has now become one of the tenth most active public chains in spot trading, surpassing projects such as Unichain and Optimism. 6. Driven by the demand for 24/7 crude oil trading, commodity perpetual contracts currently account for approximately 30% of Hyperliquid's total open interest. Since the launch of the first Hyperliquid Improvement Proposal (HIP-3) commodity perpetual contract on December 12, 2025, the open interest (OI) of HIP-3 contracts has grown exponentially, currently accounting for approximately 30% of Hyperliquid's total open interest. HIP-3 allows independent developers who stake at least 500,000 HYPE tokens to issue their own perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid. Notably, this mechanism initially supported tokenized stock trading, and later expanded to commodity contracts, a model that proved highly successful in the gold and silver markets of early 2026. Amidst heightened global uncertainty and continued tensions in the Middle East, demand for 24/7 crude oil trading has exploded, with open interest and trading volume hitting record highs for several consecutive weeks. At the end of the first quarter, open interest reached a peak of $2.1 billion, and on April 6, 2026, it broke that record again, reaching $2.3 billion. On April 9, 2026, the combined daily trading volume of tradeXYZ's two major crude oil perpetual contracts, WTIOIL and BRENTOIL, surpassed $4 billion, exceeding Bitcoin's daily trading volume on the Hyperliquid platform for the first time. Currently, tradeXYZ, the largest HIP-3 project, accounts for approximately 25.5% of Hyperliquid's open interest.

data.title: Blockchain Forum 2026 Brings Together 16,388 Participants in Moscow
data.description:
Moscow, April 14–15, 2026 — Blockchain Forum 2026 took place at Crocus Expo, becoming the largest event of its kind in Russia, one of the top two globally, and one of the most prestigious international gatherings in the fields of crypto, digital assets, and artificial intelligence.

The forum brought together 16388 participants, reinforcing its role as a key platform where market trends are shaped and the decisions defining the near future of the industry are discussed.

A major highlight of this year’s forum was the high-level dialogue between crypto businesses and government institutions. For the first time, such a scale of regulatory discussions took place within a single event. Representatives of the Central Bank of Russia, the State Duma, the Ministry of Energy, the Ministry of Digital Development, and other government bodies participated in panels focused on regulation, infrastructure development, and the integration of digital assets into the broader economy.

The stage featured leading figures from across the global industry — founders of crypto projects, top executives of major companies, investors, and public sector representatives. The event also brought together leaders from prominent Russian IT and fintech companies, including Yandex, Sber, Alfa-Bank, T-Bank, and MTS.

One of the key highlights was a talk by Errol Musk — entrepreneur, investor in AI and digital assets, and father of Elon Musk. His strategic perspective on technological development and market evolution attracted strong attention from the audience.

Garrett Bullish, one of the largest investors in crypto assets, also shared his outlook on the current market cycle and the key factors shaping its future.

The business program also featured the first large-scale AI Future Forum — a dedicated track exploring the role of artificial intelligence in shaping the future digital economy both in Russia and globally. Speakers included representatives from Rosatom, NTI, the State Duma, and other key institutions.

Beyond the conference program, Blockchain Forum once again proved to be a high-level networking platform. Over two days, participants held dozens of meetings and negotiations, forming new partnerships and business connections. The forum combined a strong conference agenda, an expo, and a professional environment for direct interaction between key market players.

Blockchain Forum proved to be:

— a meeting point for major investors, funds, and top companies
— a platform where deals are made and partnerships are formed
— a leading networking hub for the industry in the region

The event concluded with an official AfterParty, where participants continued networking in a more informal setting. The evening featured a live performance by L’One.

We would also like to highlight the companies that made the forum possible.

Promminer — an ecosystem of industrial mining solutions, including equipment supply, data center construction, its own mining pool, and advanced monitoring systems.

CoinW — a cryptocurrency exchange operating since 2017, offering spot and futures trading, Earn products, and ETF trading.

The next Blockchain Life event will take place on December 1–2, 2026, in Dubai, once again bringing together key players from the global market.

At the same time, registration is already open for the next Blockchain Forum in Moscow, where the industry will continue to shape its agenda and build strategic partnerships.

Tickets and participation opportunities are available on the official website: https://blockchain-life.com

data.title: Ukrainian Hacker Group's Mastermind Faked His Own Death—Lived a Double Life Until His $11M Crypto Trail Gave Him Away
data.description:
Hacker's Double Life Unravels

A cybercrime behind a $100 million fraud went to extraordinary lengths to destroy his former life—allegedly faking his own death and starting over a new leaf in a foreign country under a new identity—thinking he will never be discovered.

Ukrainian law enforcement authorities have achieved a significant breakthrough in international cybercrime investigations by arresting a key member of a sophisticated criminal network responsible for over $100 million in damages across the United Stateshttps://t.co/hewS09u6wOpic.twitter.com/jFdQl4Z9nL

— BitcoinWorld Media (@ItsBitcoinWorld) April 16, 2026

For years, the suspect successfully evaded authorities by constructing a false narrative of his own death, complete with fabricated documents designed to convince investigators he was gone for good. Instead, he resurfaced in Ukraine’s Transcarpathia region, quietly building a new life under an assumed identity.

According to officials, the man—wanted by the Federal Bureau of Investigation—managed to stay hidden in plain sight, using forged paperwork to operate as a completely different person while continuing to benefit from illicit proceeds.

Investigators revealed that the suspect was a central figure in a sophisticated cybercrime syndicate that targeted victims across the United States and Europe. The group deployed malware to harvest sensitive personal and corporate data, later turning that information to extort victims for large sums of money in exchange for their silence.

🚨A MUSICIAN JUST LOST $424,000 IN BITCOIN.. NOT FROM A PHISHING LINK.. NOT FROM A SKETCHY WEBSITE.. FROM AN APP HE DOWNLOADED ON APPLE’S OFFICIAL APP STORE..

Garrett Dutton.. Lead singer of G. Love & Special Sauce.. Downloaded what he thought was “Ledger Live” from the Mac App… https://t.co/u9gg03ZtCDpic.twitter.com/jZF4M7jxJW

— Evan Luthra (@EvanLuthra) April 13, 2026

The illusion of a “new life” began to collapse when financial inconsistencies and suspicious asset flows drew the attention of Ukrainian authorities. Following a coordinated investigation, law enforcement tracked the suspect to the small city of Uzhhorod, where he was ultimately apprehended.

Authorities seized approximately $11 million in assets linked to the suspect, including cash, luxury items, real estate, and around $3 million in cryptocurrency. Investigators believe the funds were laundered through a network of intermediaries, including relatives, to disguise ownership and obscure the money trail.

🚨$100M CRYPTO LAUNDERING RING BUSTED - ARREST IN UKRAINE

Authorities in Ukraine detained a member of an international hacker group behind a massive crypto laundering operation

Investigators say the group hacked companies, demanded ransom, then funneled funds into crypto, later… pic.twitter.com/LL6AaT7SwW

— Flippix (@Flippix_sol) April 16, 2026

Officials also flagged the discrepancies between the declared income and assets held by suspects where more than ten of millions of Ukrainian Hryvnias were unaccounted for.

This evidence threat also helps the authorities to reconstruct parts of the laundering network and confirm the scale of the operation. His alleged accomplices have also been charged and remain in custody.

Ukraine Bust Another Hacker Organisation

Earlier this year, authorities in Ukraine, the United States, and Germany dismantled another cross-border hacking syndicate that crippled the systems of at least 11 American companies while demanding cryptocurrency ransoms.

Prosecutor General Ruslan Kravchenko said the campaign caused roughly $1.5 million in damages and involved a network of more than 20 individuals, including seven operating from Ukraine.

Law enforcement raids on two Ukrainian suspects led to the seizure of computers, mobile devices, cash, and key documents, with investigators also linking one of the individuals to the spread of BlackBasta malware.

data.title: Some concerns and suggestions regarding Claude's strict control over Chinese users
data.description:
Author: Meng Yan

Recently, Anthropic announced further strengthening of KYC requirements and stricter restrictions on access to certain regions, sparking increasing discussion among Chinese users. Domestic reactions to this matter are roughly divided. Some worry that this will make it more difficult for large Chinese model companies to continue taking the shortcut of model distillation. Others worry that if domestic developers find it increasingly difficult to consistently use Claude Code, they will gradually fall behind in AI programming.

... Some people feel the problem isn't so serious, after all, there's more than one Claude in the world, and China's own large-scale models are also very strong. "Can't we eat mixed-breed pork without Zhang the butcher?" These thoughts are understandable, but frankly, I have another concern, and this concern is perhaps more worthy of serious consideration than "lacking a model" or "lacking a programming tool." What I worry about is that Chinese entrepreneurs, managers, and innovators are being gradually isolated from a thriving Claude intelligent agent ecosystem. This isn't simply an access issue, nor is it just a programming tool issue; it's a question about how to work with AI in the future, how to organize AI, and how to manage AI intelligence teams. I live overseas and have free access to the Claude platform and ecosystem. For some time now, I've been seriously exploring how to build, manage, cultivate, and iterate a team of truly capable AI agents. This isn't about having AI chat with me or occasionally write me a piece of copy; it's about having them function like a small organization, collaborating around a clear goal, continuously handling tasks, and gradually taking on work that originally required human intervention. In this process, I've increasingly felt that Claude's importance goes beyond simply having a "strong model" or "good coding skills." Claude Code, officially defined, is already an agentic coding tool. What's formed around it isn't just a product, but a whole set of increasingly mature workbenches, methodologies, experiences, and discussion environments. If in the past few years, many people understood AI primarily as a smarter search box or a more proficient chatbot, I now increasingly feel that in the next few years, the truly important question will become something else entirely: Can we organize AI? Can we manage an AI team? Can we get a group of agents to work stably for us? In this regard, the Claude ecosystem, at least for now, is already quite advanced. I. Why I Started Researching "AI Intelligent Agent Teams" Recently, I've been trying to build two AI intelligent agent systems. One is investment-oriented, mainly because the development of RWA allows users familiar with blockchain to freely buy and sell on-chain US stocks and ETFs. Therefore, I hope to use an AI intelligent agent system to build an investment advisory team for me, helping me with industry and company analysis, taking into account macroeconomics, news, and market sentiment, and managing my positions. I have no prior experience in investing in US stocks, and I hope this system will help me avoid some pitfalls. The other system, more focused on financial management, helps me handle ongoing financial matters such as expense tracking, account changes, investment record compilation, optimization of medical and auto insurance, and applications for various government subsidies. It generates periodic observation reports and reminds me of matters requiring my personal judgment. To be honest, neither of these systems is fully developed yet and not ready to be shown to others. However, I gained two insights during the process. The first insight is that the truly important issue is no longer "how to effectively use one AI," but rather "how to organize and manage a group of AIs." Specifically, how to assign different intelligent agents different roles, coordinate with each other, and know when to look up information, when to call tools, when to pass the results to another intelligent agent for further processing, when to stop and wait for confirmation, and when to remember what has happened for future use. This is entirely the job of a team manager. In the past year, this topic has been discussed in both the Chinese and English-speaking worlds. When OpenClaw became popular in China, many people in the Chinese community enthusiastically discussed "one-person companies." Internationally, there has also been a great deal of discussion and practice regarding "zero-human companies." These concepts sound novel and eye-catching, but in my view, they are actually just special forms of AI agent organizations. An AI agent organization is a collaborative organization composed of several AI agents with specific goals and tasks. It doesn't necessarily have to be a company. It doesn't even have to be a fixed structure. It can be a small team temporarily assembled around a specific task, with a few resident agents, or it may temporarily bring in external agents to supplement certain capabilities. Sometimes it's like a department, sometimes like a project team, and sometimes it's just an automatically operating task force. In more professional terms, it's essentially close to what people often call a multi-agent system. I just prefer to use the term "agent organization" because it makes it easier for non-technical people to grasp the key point immediately: what we are facing is no longer just a smart tool, but a collaborative structure that can be designed, trained, managed, and iterated. Once you start to understand the problem as "organizing a group of AIs," the way you view many tools will completely change. You'll no longer just care about whether it can write, answer, or search. You'll start caring about a different set of questions. Can it perform tasks long-term? Can it reliably call external tools? Can it handle information chaos as context grows longer? Can it cooperate with other intelligent agents? Can it recover after failure? Can it maintain a largely controllable state after multiple rounds of continuous work? The second takeaway is that Claude is truly strong in terms of intelligent agent ecosystems. Claude's leading ecosystem isn't just about "models feeling smarter," but rather that it's creating an ecosystem perfectly suited for researching and practicing intelligent agent organization. Speaking solely of the Claude model itself, setting aside Anthropic's masterful "scare tactics" marketing, many have commented that it's not actually that outstanding, at least not significantly superior to ChatGPT and Gemini. However, the Claude ecosystem extends beyond the model itself; it encompasses a growing number of products, documentation, interfaces, methods, and discussions related to agent workflows. Anthropic not only explicitly positions Claude Code as an agentic coding tool but also continuously provides related capabilities and documentation for agent workflows. This environment isn't just meaningful for programmers. If only programmers benefited, the impact would be limited, as only a small minority need to write code and develop software. The truly significant aspect is that the Claude ecosystem is gradually transforming capabilities originally belonging to professional developers into something accessible, understandable, and usable by non-technical personnel. In the current AI ecosystem, this not only makes it a leader but its advantage is also expanding. If Chinese users, especially entrepreneurs, managers, and startup founders who don't understand programming but desperately need to learn "how to organize AI," cannot stably enter this ecosystem in the long term, then what we lose is probably not just a tool, but a training ground. II. What is Agentic AI, and what is "AI Agent Organization"? After I started building those two agents, I found myself frequently stuck on a fundamental question: What am I actually using? How is it fundamentally different from the ChatGPT and Claude's ordinary dialogue modes we used in the past? Later, after carefully reading some materials, especially Andrew Ng's famous Agentic AI course, I finally understood this matter more clearly. Simply put, Agentic AI isn't a "question and answer" chatbot. It's more like a system that takes steps, proactively uses tools, checks intermediate results, and adjusts its approach when issues arise. For example, if you ask a regular chatbot to book a flight, it might only tell you "what flights are available from Beijing to Shanghai." An Agentic AI, however, will independently check flights, compare prices, consider timings, and confirm your preferences (window or aisle seat). If it finds direct flights too expensive, it will proactively suggest connecting flights and finally provide you with the complete itinerary and payment link. It doesn't passively answer; it acts like a reliable travel assistant, handling the entire process step-by-step in the background. Taking it a step further, we have "AI intelligent agent organization." Here, it's no longer a single agent working alone, but several agents forming a small team, each with their own tasks and collaborating with each other. For example, in my own small experiment, I can have one agent specifically responsible for collecting the latest financial information, another agent responsible for data analysis and risk assessment, another agent responsible for generating reports and checking the accuracy of the figures, and finally another agent responsible for summarizing the reports in my preferred tone and sending them to my email. They will exchange information, remind each other, and discuss solutions together when problems are discovered. Together, it looks like a small finance team, except that all members are AI. Why is this particularly important for entrepreneurs, managers, and startup founders? Because it excels at solving the "organizational problems" that are most troublesome in our daily work—how to break down processes, how to assign tasks, who is responsible for tracking, how to review problems, and how to ensure that long-term issues are not overlooked. These are precisely the most time-consuming and energy-intensive parts of traditional management. Now, AI-powered intelligent agent organizations can take over these repetitive, structured tasks, allowing us to dedicate our energy to what truly requires human judgment and creativity. I've gradually formed a not-so-shocking, but rather important, judgment: A crucial capability for future leaders will likely not just be the ability to use AI, or even just a basic understanding of AI, but the ability to create, manage, cultivate, and iterate AI-powered intelligent agent organizations. It may become as essential a skill for managers as using spreadsheets or conducting video conferences today. Not because it sounds advanced, but because it genuinely frees our time and attention from mundane tasks. This may sound a bit ahead of its time, but upon closer examination, it's not an exaggeration. Over twenty years ago, whether a manager could use email, spreadsheets, or process workflows in information systems was considered by some to be a "technical question." Today we all know that these things have long since become part of management capabilities. I believe that AI-powered intelligent agent organizations will follow a similar path. III. The Claude ecosystem is becoming a leadership training ground for "intelligent agent organizations." I increasingly value Claude, not just because of its strong models or outstanding coding capabilities. More importantly, it is gradually growing into a suitable platform for researching and training "intelligent agent organizations." This is primarily reflected in its self-positioning. In its official documentation, Anthropic directly defines Claude Code as an "agentic coding tool." The official product page goes even further: internally, most code is already written using Claude Code, allowing engineers to focus more on architecture, product thinking, and the continuous orchestration of multiple agents. This statement is quite intriguing. It's not just about "AI writing code," but rather "humans becoming organizers and coordinators of intelligent agents." First, the Claude ecosystem is no longer a single dialogue model. It continues to expand around agent workflows, with products and features converging in the same direction: making it easier to define tasks, assign roles, set memory boundaries, handle errors, and achieve long-term execution. The focus is no longer just on "dialogue capabilities," but on "how to organize a group of AIs to work." What interests me even more is that Claude's programming advantages are naturally spilling over into the advantages of intelligent agent organization. It has attracted a large number of programmers and developers who have accumulated a wealth of practical engineering experience over the past few years—such as how to encapsulate complex agent skills into reusable modules (harness engineering), how to manage long contexts, how to design error recovery mechanisms, and how to orchestrate multi-step workflows. These methods, originally circulating only within a small circle of programmers, are now being refined, packaged, and abstracted into more easily understandable forms, gradually spreading to a wider community. These methods are helping non-technical users to master intelligent agents step by step. Claude's value lies not only in making programmers write code faster, but also in gradually transforming the experience that was originally difficult to transfer from the programmer world into capabilities that non-technical managers can also gradually utilize. For example, I myself, an entrepreneur who doesn't write any code, can now use the interface and documentation provided by Claude to define a complete process of "first checking data, then analyzing risks, and finally generating reports and issuing alerts," without having to build the underlying logic from scratch. Because so many developers are continuously experimenting, summarizing, and sharing, Claude's intelligent agent ecosystem has formed a stronger positive feedback loop: the more active the ecosystem, the faster the evolution, and the more positive the interaction; the more positive the interaction, the more excellent practices are attracted, and the clearer the methodology becomes. This is a self-reinforcing cycle, and this cycle is currently running particularly smoothly at Claude. This is especially important for non-technical managers. What we really need is often not maximum freedom, but rather a sufficiently mature training ground with abundant case studies and clear methodologies. Here, you can first observe how others do it, then gradually try it out in your own business scenarios, learning as you go, without having to start from scratch every time. Therefore, I increasingly feel that Claude's greatest strength isn't necessarily just its model capabilities, but rather its ability to transform the agent engineering methods accumulated in the programmer world into organizational capabilities that non-technical managers can gradually master. This is what truly makes me believe it's becoming a leading training ground for "AI agent organization." Some might say, "For AI agent organization, you can use OpenClaw, and the domestically developed Manus is also unparalleled in agent applications. Is it necessary to praise Claude so much?" I think both OpenClaw and Manus are very successful. OpenClaw has allowed many Chinese users to truly experience for the first time that AI can not only chat, but can also actually perform tasks for humans. Manus is also a good example, demonstrating that Chinese teams are not lacking in creativity when it comes to agent productization. Furthermore, Manus provides APIs, integration, and custom connectivity capabilities, making it not a completely closed product. However, I increasingly feel that products and ecosystems are ultimately not the same thing. The problem with OpenClaw isn't that it's weak, but that it's more like a system for powerful users. Its official target users are clearly defined as developers and power users. It requires users to deploy, configure, and handle permission boundaries and access issues themselves. Such things are certainly valuable, but they are naturally more suitable for those willing to tinker, rather than a training ground where ordinary entrepreneurs, managers, and non-technical enthusiasts can easily enter and continuously practice "how to organize an AI team." Moreover, judging from the discussion enthusiasm in the Chinese community, the current wave of enthusiasm for OpenClaw has subsided quickly. According to the discussion enthusiasm index released by WeChat, the discussion enthusiasm for OpenClaw in early April was less than a quarter of that a month ago. I tend to think that this is related to its high threshold, complex installation and configuration, and numerous permission requirements, as well as the relatively fragmented nature of the domestic ecosystem itself. Major companies are all promoting their own solutions, fighting their own battles, and there is not enough interoperability, making it difficult to form a common training ground. Manus is a bit different. Its problem is not that it cannot be developed, but that, at least so far, it is more of a powerful product being used. It has the capability, the interface, and the potential, but it has not yet attracted a sufficiently dense concentration of developers and methodological accumulation like Claude, nor has it clearly developed the "AI intelligent agent organization" into a complete ecosystem axis. Products can be replaced, but training grounds are not; entry points can be replaced, but closed-loop ecosystems are not. OpenClaw and Manus both deserve our attention and support, but at least today, they are insufficient to replace the value of the Claude ecosystem in "cultivating the organizational capabilities of non-technical managers." This gap is not a matter of the strength of a single function, but a comprehensive difference in the maturity of the entire ecosystem, the speed of methodology development, and accessibility for non-technical users. Fourth, what I'm truly worried about is not the lack of a tool, but that Chinese managers will miss this learning curve. If it were just a matter of lacking a tool, the problem wouldn't be so significant. Tools are always updated, and there will always be substitutes. Today this one is stronger, tomorrow that one is stronger—this is the norm in the tech world. What I'm really worried about is something else: in the next few years, what will truly differentiate us might not be "who uses AI first," but rather "who learns to organize AI earlier." Financial management and household management are just small experiments I'm currently conducting. Moving forward, these multi-agent systems will almost certainly increasingly enter real-world business processes. Sales lead screening, customer service, investment research support, financial organization, recruitment collaboration, legal screening, and internal operations—these tasks all have one thing in common: they are not single steps, but a series of interconnected actions. Because of this, they are naturally suited to be handled by organized intelligent agents, rather than relying solely on a chatbot for temporary responses. Anthropic's continued investment in managed agents, long-running agents, and multi-agent systems demonstrates that this path is not a fringe play, but a rapid move towards a more mature workflow model. Looking further ahead, AI agent organizations won't remain at the stage of "one person managing a group of agents" for long. It will likely evolve rapidly into a more open agent network. At that point, a task may not be completed solely by agents within the same company or team, but rather collaboratively by agents from different entities, platforms, and specializations. They will communicate, exchange results, and utilize each other's capabilities; in some scenarios, mechanisms for mutual payment, authorization, and contract formation may even gradually develop. Anthropic's public research on multi-agent systems already discusses structures where multiple agents work collaboratively through cyclical tools. Once this stage is reached, competition will no longer be limited to individual models, products, or teams, but will begin to exhibit significant network effects. Those who enter such networks first will more easily accumulate interfaces, collaborative relationships, task routing, and operational experience. At that stage, catching up will be far more difficult than it is today. This is why I think Claude is more than just a useful model. It's providing not just functionality, but an increasingly mature learning environment. Others are already experimenting and discussing how to build agent teams, how to handle long-term memory, how to process context, how to design roles, and how to integrate skills and tools into real-world processes. If we are absent for a long time, we may not see drastic losses in the short term, but in the long run, we are likely to fall behind in terms of case studies, methodologies, and organizational experience. Therefore, my concern is not that Chinese users will lack an AI tool, but whether Chinese-speaking entrepreneurs, managers, and startup founders will fall behind on this crucial learning curve. V. Several Suggestions This article is not intended to create anxiety, but rather to remind everyone to pay attention. To solve a problem, you must first recognize its existence. And once the problem is identified, there are always solutions. I believe there are a few things that can be done. First, at the individual level, conduct some small-scale multi-agent experiments as early as possible. Don't just treat AI as a chat tool, but try to have two or three agents work together to complete a small process—even if it's just helping you organize weekly reports, track project progress, or manage your personal knowledge base. Taking action is more valuable than just reading others' sharing. Second, at the management level, I suggest that managers personally try it out. This is not a purely technical issue, but a future organizational issue. Only by building, debugging, and iterating on the platform ourselves can we truly understand the changes that an AI agent team can bring, and better lead the team to complete the cognitive upgrade from "human-managed people" to "human-managed AI teams." Third, at the learning level, instead of being obsessed with a single product, it's better to prioritize learning transferable methods. Focus on core capabilities such as workflow design, task allocation logic, memory management, result verification, error recovery, and multi-agent collaboration. These methods, regardless of the platform ultimately used, can help us build our own agent organization capabilities more quickly. Fourth, at the industry level, large domestic model companies should step forward and take on this responsibility. Since Anthropic's agent ecosystem excludes Chinese users, large domestic model companies have a responsibility to build an equally prosperous ecosystem in China.

data.title: a16z: From retail traders to top institutions, predicting the market is maturing.
data.description:
Authors: Alex Immerman, Santiago Rodriguez, Partners at a16z Growth Fund; Source: a16z; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

The financial sector is highly segmented, with each segment boasting its own recognized top-tier benchmark conferences. Leaders in healthcare services, payers, and biotechnology gather annually in San Francisco for the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference. Global macroeconomic giants and politicians travel to the Swiss Alps each year for the Davos Forum. Technology, media, telecommunications, real estate, industry, financial services, and virtually every other sector have their own signature summits.

In late March, Kalshi Research, Kalshi's division focusing on academic and institutional research, held its first research conference in New York, bringing together academic experts, Wall Street executives, former politicians, and traders who influence market trends. The composition of the attendees perfectly illustrates that "this industry is maturing." The conference opened with a dialogue between Kalshi co-founders Tarek Mansour and Luana Lopes Lara and Bloomberg reporter Katherine Doherty. Below are some key viewpoints on industry development from this dialogue and subsequent roundtable discussions.

1. Life (and Markets) is More Than Just Elections and Sports

Major news cycles often exhibit a pattern: a hot event (such as the 2024 election, the Super Bowl, or the recent NCAA March Madness basketball tournament) dominates headlines, driving up prediction market trading volume and creating the illusion that "prediction markets can only do that."

However, although early views generally held that prediction markets were only viable during election cycles, Kalshi...

... Significant growth has already been achieved in other areas. During this research conference, weekly sports transactions approached $3 billion, accounting for approximately 80% of Kalshi's total transactions, primarily driven by the NCAA March Madness basketball tournament. Tarek Mansour and Luana Lopes Lara define the dominance of the sports category as a temporary phenomenon. More relevant data shows that even though the absolute transaction volume of sports transactions reached a record high, its share of total transaction volume is actually at a historical low. All other categories are growing faster. They point out that entertainment, cryptocurrency, and politics & culture are showing stronger user growth and better transaction retention than sports. Sports act as a catalyst for the mass market, serving as a familiar, regularly scheduled, and emotionally driven product for attracting users. However, the company also achieved significant growth in the long tail market, which accounts for more than 20% of Kalshi's remaining trading volume and is crucial for institutional hedging and the information market. A subsequent institutional panel discussion corroborated this view from the demand side: Cyril Goddeeris, co-head of global equities at Goldman Sachs, stated that forecasts related to macroeconomic events and CPI data are the most closely watched areas on Wall Street. Sally Shin, executive vice president of growth at CNBC, said she has been using forecasts related to the Federal Reserve Chairman's market and non-farm payroll data as a narrative tool. Troy Dixon, co-head of global markets at Tradeweb, envisions a future where large Wall Street banks will establish dedicated prediction market trading departments, with financial contracts as their core product. 2. Why Kalshi Gains Wall Street's Attention Traditional financial markets function for many reasons, a key one being the existence of universally accepted benchmarks for each major asset class: the S&P 500 is the average performance of 500 stocks; crude oil has a benchmark price set by the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). However, **in terms of political and economic events** (such as who wins an election, whether a tariff bill passes, or the outcome of a Supreme Court case), **previously there were almost no widely accepted and dynamically updated benchmarks. Prediction markets have changed this.** Today, the future trajectory of almost any event has a real-time, liquid market benchmark. Once a credible price is available for an event, such as a quote indicating the probability of a 30% tariff bill passing, institutional counterparties can trade at that price. This creates a mechanism for directly trading events or hedging downside risk in other parts of a portfolio. As Tradeweb's Troy Dixon stated: "Recall when Trump was first elected; there was a lot of hedging activity in the stock market. The strategy was to short the S&P 500 because it was widely believed that a Trump victory would cause the stock market to fall. This trade resulted in huge losses. The core problem is: how do you price these kinds of events? What is the benchmark?" Tarek Mansour also mentioned a similar motivation for founding Kalshi: he previously worked in Goldman Sachs' trading department, where the team's recommended trades were linked to the 2024 US election and Brexit. Without market prediction, institutions attempting to hedge political or macroeconomic event risks with related assets are essentially placing two bets simultaneously: one on the outcome of the event itself, and the other on the correlation between the event and the traded asset. The second bet could potentially fail independently. With a direct benchmark for the event itself, these two bets become one. As Tarek Mansour says, "This market group is now pricing in all kinds of events." 3. Three Stages of Large-Scale Institutional Adoption It's premature to say that large Wall Street institutions are already trading large amounts on Kalshi: currently, institutions mostly use Kalshi as a data source rather than a trading platform. However, Luana Lopes Lara states that the path for Wall Street to more broadly access this market is very clear, summarizing it in three stages: Stage One: Data Application Integrating price data into institutional workflows, until Goldman Sachs portfolio managers habitually browse Kalshi's probability quotes like they would the VIX volatility index, has become a reality to some extent. As Johns Hopkins University professor and former Federal Reserve official Jonathan Wright stated, "Kalshi is almost a unique source of reference for data such as Fed decisions, unemployment rates, and GDP." **Phase Two: System Integration** Completing compliance and legal approvals, technical system integration, and internal staff training—the entire process of introducing a new financial instrument. **Phase Three: Practical Implementation** Actual risk hedging on exchanges, with trading volume and market depth beginning a positive cycle. At this stage, more hedging positions attract more speculative positions, narrower bid-ask spreads attract more hedgers, and the market benchmark becomes self-reinforcing. Currently, most institutions are still in Phase 1, a significant number have entered Phase 2, and only a few have reached Phase 3. A major reason why many institutions have failed to enter Phase 3 is that current market prediction contracts require the full notional value as margin – a $100 position requires $100 to be deposited with the clearinghouse. This is acceptable for retail traders, but a significant limitation for hedge funds or banks that rely on leverage and return on capital. Tarek Mansour stated, “If you want to hedge $100, you have to deposit $100 with a clearinghouse. That’s too expensive for institutions. Companies like Citadel Investments and Millennium Management wouldn’t do that.” Kalshi recently obtained a license from the National Futures Association (NFA) and is working with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to push for margin trading. 4. Future Direction Bloomberg’s Head of Market Innovation, Michael McDonough, put it most bluntly: “Success means these products become commonplace.” He likened it to the options market in the 1970s: when concerns about market manipulation and regulatory uncertainty were similar, these issues were eventually resolved as infrastructure improved, becoming so routine that no one paid much attention anymore. Toby Moskowitz, a partner at AQR Management, said he is “betting real money” that the market will become a mature tool available to institutions within five years—or even sooner. Garrett Herren of the voting platform Vote Hub described the final state of maturity: “The question of the future will no longer be whether we should use prediction markets, but how we should use them. Once people start asking this question, it means it has become indispensable.” In fact, although prediction markets are currently small, the hedging market itself is extremely large: [Image of prediction markets] In fact, the normalization of prediction markets has already begun. During the political panel discussion, former Congressman Mondaire Jones pointed out that top figures in both parties' Congress—including President Trump, House Minority Leader Jeffries, and Senate Minority Leader Schumer—have publicly cited Kalshi's probability data. DDHQ's Scott Tranter confirmed that prediction market data has now become a standard reference indicator within party committees. Vote Hub also announced that it will directly integrate Kalshi data into its midterm election prediction model. All of this was unimaginable two years ago. Two years ago, the most successful traders on Kalshi were just a group of amateurs. Now the situation is different, and calling them "amateurs" is no longer appropriate. In Kalshi's panel discussion, "Traders Behind the Markets," four traders shared their experiences making a living from this field, their professional habits mirroring those of professional traders—one having dedicated eleven years to studying Billboard data, and another having entered the prediction market as early as 2006, when it was merely a "nerdy hobby" without real financial involvement. None of the four panelists had a financial background; they came from the fields of music, politics, and poker, respectively, but they unanimously agreed that the platform truly rewards deep expertise in the field, not impressive credentials. Prediction markets have come a long way. Initially a novel concept in academia, they became a hot topic during elections, and later a derivative of sports betting. This conference clearly demonstrated that prediction markets are maturing into an infrastructure providing pricing for uncertainty, serving participants from retail traders to top institutions, with increasingly diverse applications.

data.title: Anthropic's latest Opus 4.7 model features 8 Hidden Blades.
data.description:
Author: Alan Walker, Silicon Valley

The press conference shone the spotlight on the SWE-bench, but the real signals were hidden in the footnotes, the introductory blocks, and an inconspicuous phrase like "auto mode."

On California Ave in Palo Alto, the light of 9:30 in the morning swept obliquely through the glass windows of Coupa Café, illuminating Alan Walker's half-cold flat white. He had just finished browsing Anthropic's website, leaned back in his chair, and began to speak to Tony, who had just sat down opposite him.

"Anthropic's Opus 4.7 launch event was quite restrained—the main focus was on the SWE-bench pillars, the customer quote slideshow, and a beautiful alignment diagram. Most tech media simply copied the press release and left." "But the real substance is buried in the footnotes, the migration guide, and a casual mention of 'auto mode extended to Max users.' You have to read it like a 10-K report—the main text is for retail investors, the footnotes are for institutions." "Before I finish this cup of coffee today, I'll disassemble eight knives. Each one will tell you who it's going to cut." (Image of a 7453650 image) "xhigh is not a gear upgrade — Default has been secretly increased." (Image of a 7453650 image) The press conference briefly mentioned: "In Claude Code, we've raised the default effort level to xhigh for all plans." Most people, seeing "xhigh," assume it means "another level has been added," like adding a new color to an iPhone. Wrong. The real signal is in the last half of the sentence—the default level for all plans in Claude Code has been raised to xhigh. This is a very Anthropic move: quietly raising everyone's baseline by one level, while keeping the computing power bill unchanged. It's like giving you a smarter colleague, but without a raise.

TONY: Wait, doesn't this mean that Pro users who used to spend $20 for medium can now directly get xhigh?

ALAN: Yes. And read that Hex quote carefully—"low-effort 4.7 ≈ medium-effort 4.6". With the default tier boosted, the effective intelligence that ordinary users get jumps two whole tiers. The press conference didn't capitalize this number because they didn't want the token consumption page to look bad. ◆ Application Scenarios On Monday morning, you ask Claude Code to modify a 500-line backend module—before, you had to manually type `/effort max` before you dared to let it run automatically; now you don't need to configure anything, it defaults to xhigh, and you can finish the work after a cup of coffee. This difference isn't just 10% faster, it's "you don't need to worry about it anymore."

 KILL LIST

→ "AI Tuning / prompt configuration" type SaaS——Those tools that teach you how to adjust thinking budget and how to select effort, the default values ​​are automatically correct, the middle layer has no business

→ Junior Engineer Position——xhigh's default work is already the quality lower limit of a three-year experienced engineer

→ Outsourced Code Review Company——The third knife below will kill this

——BLADE NO. 02

Auto Mode —— Permission UI The Silent Revolution

The third line of the press conference footnote: "Auto mode extended to Max users." Just one sentence.

Anthropic's official website quote: "auto mode is a new permissions option where Claude makes decisions on your behalf."——"Make decisions on your behalf."

In the past year, all agent startups have been caught between two extremes: either skip-all-permissions (the Devin, Cognition path), or bombarding users with approval/deny pop-ups (early Cursor). Anthropic took a third path: training the model to judge for itself what should and shouldn't be asked, and internalizing this judgment into auto mode.

KAI: Alan, what's the fundamental difference between this and skip permissions? Aren't they both just letting it run?

ALAN: The difference is huge. Skip is like pulling the safety plug; you're responsible if something goes wrong. Auto is like the model installing its own safety mechanism—it proactively stops and asks you for help with dangerous operations, and handles low-risk operations itself. Essentially, it moves the entire "permission UI" layer from the product's outer shell to the model's weights. TONY: So those YC startups making "agent governance/guardrails"... ALAN: The product is essentially built into the model. This is a perfect example of what Andrej said last year: "the model is the product."

KILL LIST

→Agent guardrails / approval-flow SaaS——Those who make "human-machine collaborative approval platforms" have had their entire category downgraded

→RPA Traditional Industries (UiPath / Automation Anywhere)——Their core value is "controllable automation," and now controllability is inherent

→BPO Outsourcing Industry Back-office Operations——Data entry, customer service assignment, invoice reconciliation in the Philippines and India, running in auto mode for a whole day is the work of an entire team

——BLADE NO. 03

/ultrareview—An Assassination Order for Senior Engineers

Official wording: "a dedicated review session that reads through changes and flags bugs and design issues that a careful reviewer would catch."

Note that word—"a careful reviewer." Not junior, not linter, but "careful reviewer." In layman's terms: senior engineer.

CodeRabbit's David Loker provides even more direct figures: recall increased by over 10%, uncovering the most difficult bugs in the most complex PRs, with precision almost not dropping. Increased recall and undiminished precision—in the code review field, this is the holy grail. The last person to achieve this combination was a Google internal Tricorder, who worked on it for ten years.

MARCUS: At FAANG, a staff member earns $800K a year, and half of that time is spent reviewing PRs. If this thing really works…

ALAN: Pro and Max users get three free UltraReviews, letting you try it out. This is a common Silicon Valley tactic of "freemium poisoning"—giving you a taste, then making you unable to go back. MARCUS: So this isn't a tool, it's a substitute. ALAN: Not entirely. It doesn't replace staff, it replaces the two hours staff spend reviewing ten PRs every afternoon. Those freed-up two hours are for senior engineers, not human GitHub bots. ◆ Application Scenarios: A 20-person engineering team where the tech lead used to spend three hours a day reviewing PRs. On UltraReview, the tech lead only needs to look at the "design issues" highlighted by Claude—three hours reduced to twenty minutes, the saved time actually used for architecture. This isn't "AI assistance," it's a rewriting of job responsibilities.

KILL LIST

→ All independent AI code review startups — CodeRabbit, Codacy, Qodo, are now features of Anthropic

→ Traditional security scanning tools (Snyk/Checkmarx) — Rule-driven static scanning is crushed by the "read code like a human" approach

→ Outsourced code review services in India/Eastern Europe — This market, valued at billions of dollars over the past decade, has now evaporated

—BLADE NO. 04

"The longest side of the acceptable image is now 2,576 pixels, approximately 3.75 megapixels, more than three times the previous size."

This is the most underestimated point. Most people see it and think, "Oh, it's higher resolution." That's a huge mistake. This is the watershed moment for the entire computer-use category, marking its transition from demo to production.

The evidence is in the quote at the very bottom of the release page, a quote from XBOW CEO Oege de Moor—54.5% → 98.5%. This isn't a gradual improvement; it's a leap from "unusable" to "unavoidable." Opus 4.6 was still guessing where the buttons on the screen were, while 4.7 could already read small print and nested tables on dense dashboards.

SARAH: Our enterprise clients have been stuck at this point. 4.6 When we tried to automate the processing of scanned invoices, half of them were wrong—the boss simply said, "Stop messing around."

ALAN: Now, the 98.5% accuracy rate means that RPA, IT operations, expense auditing, legacy system migration—all workflows that still rely on human visual input on screens—have, for the first time, an acceptable baseline model.

KAI: Computer use is no longer a demo video; it's productivity.

ALAN: Yes, and note—this is a model-level upgrade, not API parameters. Existing users don't need to change anything; they automatically benefit from it. Anthropic is quietly pushing the product capabilities of all integrators to a higher level.

KILL LIST

→ OCR / Document Understanding SaaS (Rossum / Hyperscience / Nanonets) —— Their moat was originally "vision + structured," but now it's being caught up with or even surpassed by general models

→ The traditional RPA giants —— UiPath's screen recognition core technology, its value evaporated by half overnight

→ Enterprise application data entry departments —— Medical insurance claims, bank KYC, government form processing, the whole process is a human assembly line

→ Independent penetration testing / red team industry —— Companies like XBOW actually benefited, but traditional Pentesting Consulting Services Exposed

—— BLADE NO. 05

File-System Memory——Anthropic Chooses the Simplest Path

A footnote at the press conference: "Opus 4.7 is better at using file system-based memory. It remembers important notes across long, multi-session work."

OpenAI is using "embedded memory"—memory embedded in the model, invisible and unchangeable. Google is working on the mysterious infini-attention. Anthropic has revealed its hand this time: 'The file system is memory.' Claude writes and reads .md notes, which you can then easily parse and view. This choice may seem low-tech, but it's actually a victory of first principles. The core issue of memory is never storage, but auditability, editability, and transferability. Vector databases and embedded memory both violate these three principles.

ERIC: The biggest fear for enterprise customers is, "What exactly has this AI remembered about me? I don't know."

ALAN: File system memory directly solves compliance. GDPR erasure rights? Just rm it. SOC2 audit? CAT it to the auditor. This isn't a technological advantage, it's a legal advantage.

ERIC:So those startups working on "AI memory layers"...

ALAN:Mem0, LangMem, Zep — raised a lot of money this year. They solved the problem of "models not managing their own memory." Anthropic has written this capability into the model, and uses the most basic POSIX file system. The intermediate layer is skipped.

KILL LIST

→ AI Memory Infrastructure Startups (Mem0 / LangMem / Zep) — Value Proposition Internalized into Models

→ Agentic Memory Use Cases for Some Vector Databases — A Major Narrative of Pinecone and Weaviate Affected

→ AI Enhancement Layer for Enterprise Knowledge Management SaaS — No More Third-Party Middleware Needed, Claude Directly Reads and Writes Project Files

—BLADE NO. 06

Task Budgets — For Agents

Install the brakes, then release the accelerator. "Giving developers a way to guide Claude's token spend so it can prioritize work across longer runs." (public beta) This was overlooked by all the media, but it's the most important engineering breakthrough for long-running agents this year. Over the past year, all agent companies have been struggling with the same demon: long-running tokens are out of control. Give Devin or Cursor a complex task, it runs for two hours, and comes back telling you it burned through $800, only half the work is done. The boss's eyes turn green when he sees the bill. The task budget design is ingenious—it's not a simple token cap, but rather, it allows the model to see the budget countdown and decide which steps to skip and how to achieve the most critical level of completion. This is exactly the "minimum deliverable" mindset in project management. Anthropic has trained the PM skill of scope-cutting into its model. Given a $10 budget to run the agent, it will automatically decide which features to complete at 80% and which must be 100%. TONY: Therefore, Notion's quote—"implicit-need tests"—is correct. ALAN: The model is starting to have "resource awareness," able to guess what you expect but haven't explicitly stated, and prioritize it within the budget. This is training "senior engineer judgment."

KILL LIST

→ AI cost-control / LLM observable startup (Helicone / Langfuse cost module)——Core functionality is nativeized

→ Agent orchestration framework (partial LangGraph / CrewAI usage)——The model can plan the budget itself, without external scheduling

→ Project management part of the traditional consulting industry——The intelligence of "resource allocation + delivery tailoring" is handled by the model

—— BLADE NO. 07

Joe Haddad, Distinguished Eng at Vercel: "It even does proofs on systems code before starting work, which is new behavior we haven't seen from earlier Claude models."

This sentence is buried among more than twenty quotes, and no one has highlighted it. But the old OG put down his coffee as soon as he read this.

"proofs on systems code"—before writing system-level code, the model will first perform mathematical/formal proofs on its own. This doesn't mean it's smarter; it means the model is starting to verify its own code using the same methods as PhD students verifying their dissertations ...>

Joe Haddad, Distinguished Eng at Vercel: "It even does proofs on systems code before starting work, which is new behavior we haven't seen from earlier Claude models."

This sentence is buried among more than twenty quotes, and no one has highlighted it. But the old OG put down his coffee as soon as he read this.

MARCUS: This behavior appears in the training data, indicating that Anthropic explicitly rewards "proof before coding" during the RL phase.

ALAN: Yes, this was consciously trained. Combining Vercel's section on "loop resistance" with Genspark's, and Hex's "correctly reports when data is missing instead of plausible-but-incorrect fallbacks"—what you see is a complete, tasteful training engineering approach: making the model work like an engineer who isn't easily fooled. MARCUS: Not easily fooled—meaning not deceiving itself. ALAN: Yes. Opus 4.7 no longer just provides a seemingly workable solution to complete a task. This is a true embodiment of alignment at the product level.

KILL LIST

→Formal Verification Tool Sub-market (Partial)—Some introductory scenarios for high-barrier tools like Coq/Lean/TLA+, where the model handles it for you

→High-Frequency Trading / Blockchain Security Audit Industry—The core work of auditors ("reading code to find invariant violations") is collaboratively handled by the model, reducing audit costs

→Operating System Kernel / Embedded System Outsourcing—Those sub-sectors requiring proof-based reasoning have had their barriers to entry lowered

— BLADE NO. 08

Cyber ​​Verification—The Window for Regulatory Arbitrage Has Been Opened

"During its training, we experimented with efforts to differentially reduce these capabilities."

The most ingenious move is here. Anthropic admits that during training, they **actively reduced the network attack and defense capabilities of Opus 4.7** because the more powerful Mythos Preview behind it wasn't released. Then—

Then they launched a **Cyber ​​Verification Program**, allowing legitimate security researchers, pentesters, and red team members to unlock higher privileges after certification.

ERIC: This…isn't this just a model version of export controls?

ALAN: More accurately, it's "Capability KYC". The model has three levels of capability gates; you need to prove your identity to unlock the corresponding level. For the first time, the window for regulatory arbitrage is being openly priced by AI companies themselves. What does this mean for startups? First, for general "AI + Security" startups aiming for high-end applications, Anthropic certification is essential, effectively controlling the supply chain. Second, a completely new category will emerge: consulting services that help you achieve Anthropic certification—similar to companies that help you pass SOC2 certification today. Third, this is Anthropic practicing how to release all future frontier models; Mythos's release will only be more stringent. Therefore, companies like Palantir and Booz Allen, with their strong government compliance backgrounds… have gained an extra layer of competitive advantage. They already possessed liquidation-level status, now naturally unlocking the top-level model. ◆ Application Scenarios A Y Combinator entrepreneur wanting to do AI pentesting will, starting in Q2 2026, have to answer on the first page of their business plan, "Have you obtained the Anthropic Cyber ​​Verification?" No? VCs won't invest. Obtained? Valuation doubled. A certification, a watershed moment in the capital market.

KILL LIST & New Track

→General Cybersecurity Startup SaaS—Without Anthropic certification, you can't get upper-level model capabilities, and your ceiling is locked

→The Emergence of a New Track: "AI Model Capability Compliance Consulting"—A Batch of Intermediaries Helping Enterprises with Frontier Model Certification Will Emerge in the Next 12 Months

→Traditional Military and Government Integrators (Palantir / Booz Allen)—Naturally Benefit, Barriers Become Moats

→Open Source / The local deployment camp—Llama, Qwen, and DeepSeek—benefited, with "usable without authentication" becoming a core selling point. Outside the window, the sun on California Ave had already climbed over the roof of Palo Alto Creamery, its rays slanting across the glass. "Eight knives, slashing in eight directions. Some tracks die today, some begin to live." "The real stuff about each generation of frontier models isn't written in the headline," he told Tony. "The launch event is for analysts. The numbers in the footnotes and quotes are what we see." "Don't just watch the show."

data.title: Charles Schwab launches cryptocurrency spot trading; Anthropic releases latest model.
data.description:
Headline

▌Charles Schwab Announces Launch of Spot Crypto Trading Service

According to the Wall Street Journal, Charles Schwab officially announced the launch of its Schwab Crypto spot cryptocurrency trading service on April 16, which will be rolled out to retail clients in phases over the next few weeks. The service supports direct trading of Bitcoin and Ethereum, and includes educational content and professional customer support, while integrating Charles Schwab's existing investment, trading, wealth management, and banking services.

▌Anthropic Releases its Latest Model OPUS 4.7

On April 16, Anthropic released its latest model, Claude Opus 4.7, which is now fully available.

▌JPMorgan Chase: Clarity Act Negotiations Enter Final Stage, Disputes Reduced to 2-3 Core Issues

On April 16, JPMorgan Chase analysts stated that negotiations on the U.S. Cryptocurrency Market Structure Act (Clarity Act) have entered the final stage, with both sides reaching compromises on the remaining few points of contention. The disputes have been reduced from over a dozen to 2-3 core issues, with discussions on stablecoin rewards "in good shape." While banks have expressed concerns about stablecoins offering deposit-like returns, the overall trend is one of bipartisan compromise. JPMorgan Chase believes that "no bill is perfect," and if passed, the act will provide significant regulatory clarity for the integration of digital assets into the U.S. financial system. The remaining major disagreements focus on stablecoin rewards, DeFi regulation, and token classification. Although optimism is rising, the bill still faces the risk of delay due to the 2026 midterm elections, potentially entering a more uncertain political environment.

... If the bill is ultimately passed, it will divide the regulatory powers of the SEC and CFTC, providing a long-term regulatory framework for stablecoins, DeFi, and the entire crypto industry.

▌Trump says next US-Iran meeting may be held this weekend

On April 17, US President Trump, speaking about the Iran issue on April 16 local time, said he was unsure whether the ceasefire needed to be extended, but that significant progress was being made. Trump also stated that the next meeting with Iran might be held this weekend. (CCTV News)

▌Trump says he may travel to Pakistan to push for an agreement with Iran

On April 17, US President Trump stated that he might travel to Pakistan to push for an agreement with Iran.

He told reporters at the White House, "If the agreement is signed in Islamabad, I might go. The (Pakistani) Field Marshal did a very good job, and the Prime Minister did a very good job. So I might go. They want me to go." Trump had previously stated that the US might hold talks with Iran this weekend, saying, "We are very close to reaching an agreement." On April 17, local time on April 16, US President Trump stated that the US had received a "very substantial" statement to ensure that Iran would not possess nuclear weapons, and that this commitment would be valid for more than 20 years. However, Trump did not disclose the details of the so-called "statement." Trump also stated that Iran had agreed not to possess nuclear weapons, and that the prospects for reaching an agreement "look very optimistic." He said, "Iran has agreed to almost all of the demands." Currently, there has been no response from Iran. (CCTV News) Trump: Lebanon and Israel Agree to Ceasefire On April 16, US President Trump posted that he had just had a very pleasant meeting with the highly respected President of Lebanon and Prime Minister of Israel, Netanyahu. The two leaders have agreed to formally begin a 10-day ceasefire at 5 p.m. Eastern Time in order to achieve peace between the two countries. On Tuesday, representatives of both countries met with our great Secretary of State, Rubio, in Washington, D.C., for the first time in 34 years. He instructed Vice President Vance, Secretary of State Rubio, and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Kaine to work with Israel and Lebanon to achieve lasting peace. It has been a great honor to resolve nine wars around the world, and this will be the tenth, so let's get this done!

▌Israeli Media: Israel Agrees to 10-Day Ceasefire but Will Not Withdraw Troops from Lebanon

On April 17, according to multiple Israeli media reports on the 16th, following a request from US President Trump, Israel has agreed to a 10-day ceasefire with Hezbollah in Lebanon, but will not withdraw its troops from southern Lebanon. Israeli public broadcaster and Channel 12 reported that Prime Minister Netanyahu convened a telephone conference with his security cabinet that day, informing them that he had agreed to Trump's ceasefire request. The reports said that Netanyahu stated during the meeting that Israeli troops will maintain their existing deployment in southern Lebanon. Israel considers this area a security "buffer zone" and stated that the current ceasefire agreement's terms allow the Israeli military to continue taking action upon detecting a "threat."

(Xinhua News Agency) Blockchain Applications ▌ETH Foundation Core Member Josh Stark Announces Resignation On April 17, Josh Stark, a core figure at the Ethereum Foundation (EF), announced his resignation after five years in office. He will officially step down at the end of the month. Stark joined the Ethereum Foundation in 2019, initially working on a special projects team before rising to leadership positions. He worked with EF Chairman Aya Miyaguchi, Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin, and co-executive directors Hsiao-Wei Wang and Bastian Aue, among other senior figures. He was one of the foundation's most prominent members, leading several major developments in Ethereum, including "The Merge," the upgrade from Proof-of-Work to Proof-of-Stake, and subsequent upgrades such as Dencun, Fusaka, and Pectra. During last year's leadership reshuffle at the Ethereum Foundation, he was appointed Co-Manager of the EF Board of Directors and recently became Co-Chair of the "Trillion Dollar Security" initiative. Last month, Stark co-authored an Ethereum strategy blog post with Josh Rudolf and Julian Ma, outlining Ethereum's latest scaling direction and its relationship with the Layer 2 ecosystem. Stark stated, "I haven't planned for the future yet; I just want to rest and spend time with family and friends." This departure comes against the backdrop of ongoing changes at the Ethereum Foundation. Last year, the foundation underwent a major leadership reshuffle, subsequently refocusing its strategic emphasis on Ethereum mainnet scaling and core cypherpunk values. Tomasz K. Stańczak also resigned as Co-Executive Director at the end of February this year, after serving less than a year. On the same day, Trent Van Epps also announced his departure from EF to dedicate himself full-time to Protocol Guild, the independent funding organization for Ethereum core developers that he founded.

▌Coinbase: Gold and Silver Futures to Trade 24/7

On April 17, Coinbase announced, "Gold and silver futures will trade 24/7. Starting May 1, these markets will be the first in U.S. history to offer 24/7 trading. As the world's oldest store of value, they will now have modern trading hours."

▌OpenAI Launches Drug Discovery AI Model to Challenge Google

On April 17, OpenAI launched an early version of an artificial intelligence (AI) model designed to accelerate drug discovery, joining tech companies in a race to prove that AI can drive more scientific breakthroughs. The ChatGPT developer said Thursday that the model, named GPT-Rosalind, is geared towards life science research, such as helping to extract insights from massive amounts of data and translating research into patient-facing medical applications.

... OpenAI stated that the model will initially be available to select enterprise clients in a research preview format. Initial users include pharmaceutical company Amgen, vaccine manufacturer Moderna, and the Allen Institute, a non-profit bioscience research organization. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Alphabet's Google are increasingly focusing on the application of AI in science and medicine, including assisting in new drug development and analyzing personal medical data.
