▌Drift: Receives $147.5 Million in Funding from Tether and Others for User Recovery

On April 16th, Drift announced that the Drift Protocol has received support from Tether and other partners, with Tether contributing $127.5 million and other partners contributing $20 million, to jointly support user recovery efforts following the April 1st attack. This support package includes a $100 million revenue-linked credit line, ecosystem grants, and loans to market makers. Drift will establish a dedicated user recovery pool, aiming to gradually resolve the $295 million in outstanding user losses as trading revenue grows. In addition, Drift will issue independent recovery tokens to affected users, representing a claim to the recovery pool and are transferable. Currently, Drift is undergoing a protocol reboot, has engaged Ottersec and Asymmetric for audits, and is migrating its settlement layer from USDC to USDT.

The previous attack resulted in the theft of assets worth approximately $295 million, but the insurance fund's assets were unaffected. On April 16th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, Abraxas Capital (Alpha Bitcoin Fund) deposited 1,993 bitcoins into Kraken, worth $148.32 million. Since March 14th, Abraxas Capital (Alpha Bitcoin Fund) has cumulatively deposited 9,582 bitcoins into Kraken, worth $691 million, and currently still holds 20,337 bitcoins, worth $1.51 billion. BlackRock Transfers 3,446 Bitcoins from Coinbase, Worth Approximately $255.2 Million On April 16th, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, BlackRock transferred 3,446 Bitcoins from Coinbase yesterday, worth approximately $255.2 million. Arthur Hayes: BTC Won't See Significant Rise Until Fed Provides Liquidity to Fill Bank Balance Sheet Gaps On April 16th, according to Cointelegraph, Arthur Hayes stated that Bitcoin (BTC) will not see a substantial rise until the Federal Reserve provides liquidity to fill the gaps in bank balance sheets. He currently holds an unleveraged long position in BTC and is bullish on HYPE amid uncertainty surrounding the Strait of Hormuz.

▌Tom Lee: BTC, ETH, and MAG7 Stocks Expected to Lead the Next Bull Market

On April 16th, according to Cointelegraph, Fundstrat's Tom Lee stated that despite the S&P 500 hitting a record high, investors remain skeptical. However, he expects Bitcoin, Ethereum, and MAG7 stocks to lead the next bull market.

▌Binance to Launch Three US Stock USDT-Margined Perpetual Contracts: Microsoft, Broadcom, and Alibaba

On April 16th, Binance will launch three US stock USDT-margined perpetual contracts: MSFTUSDT (Microsoft), AVGOUSDT (Broadcom), and BABAUSDT (Alibaba).

**Important Economic Developments** **▌US Launches Operation Codenamed "Economic Fury" Against Iran** **April 16th News:** On April 16th local time, US Defense Secretary Hergsays stated at a press conference that the US Treasury Department is launching an operation codenamed "Economic Fury" to "exert maximum economic pressure on Iran." (CCTV) **▌Torrent Fees for the Strait of Hormuz to be Paid Through Iranian Banks** **April 16th News:** According to the Iranian Students' News Agency, parliamentary officials stated that Iran plans to pay tolls for passage through the Strait of Hormuz through Iranian banks.

▌US Banking Committee Democrats: Warsh's Hearing Should Be Postponed

On April 16, Democratic members of the US Senate Banking Committee stated that the hearing for Federal Reserve Chairman nominee Kevin Warsh should be postponed.

On April 16, Federal Reserve Chairman Williams stated that the Fed's policy is currently moderately tight. Inflation will be "well above" 3% in the coming months. Uncertainty about the economic outlook limits the statements that Fed officials can make regarding the outlook for interest rate policy.

Williams said, "Given the numerous uncertainties in the current economic situation and the impact of the Middle East war, now is not the time to give clear guidance. If inflation falls back to 2%, I think lowering interest rates would be appropriate, but we are not at that stage yet." Goldman Sachs stated on April 16th that although it expects inflation to rise, economic growth to slow, and unemployment to rise slightly, the current shock is not enough to trigger a full-blown supply chain crisis, nor will it force the Federal Reserve to implement panic rate hikes. Goldman Sachs pointed out that the rise in unemployment, coupled with further improvement in core inflation, should be sufficient to offset the upward pressure from energy price transmission, which will provide strong support for rate cuts of 25 basis points in September and December respectively.

▌Cuban President Says “US Military Aggression Possible,” Urges Public to Prepare

On the 16th local time, Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel stated during an event in Havana that a “military aggression” by the United States against Cuba is possible. Díaz-Canel said that Cuba is experiencing an “absolutely challenging” moment, and the country faces an imminent “serious threat,” including “military aggression” from the United States. Díaz-Canel urged Cubans to be “prepared” to fight in the face of invasion. He said, “We do not want war, but we have a responsibility to be prepared.” Furthermore, Díaz-Canel condemned the “genocidal multi-layered blockade” imposed by the United States on Cuba, calling it the “main cause” of shortages and suffering in Cuban families, severely impacting the supply of basic necessities and development needs of the Cuban people.

(CCTV) ▌Iranian Armed Forces: Iranian Army is Fully Prepared for Defense On April 16 local time, Pakistani Army Chief of Staff Munir, during a meeting with Abdullah, Commander of the Hatem Anbia Central Command of the Iranian Armed Forces, briefed Pakistan on the measures taken to end the conflict and emphasized that these efforts would continue. Abdullah stressed, "If the enemy harbors malicious intent, the Iranian army is fully prepared for defense." (CCTV) ▌Federal Reserve's Milan: Only Three Rate Cuts Likely for the Remaining Year Federal Reserve Governor Milan stated that, currently, it appears that only three rate cuts are likely for the remaining year.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 99.5%

On April 17th, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 0.5%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 99.5%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 1.4%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 98%, and the probability of cumulatively raising interest rates by 25 basis points is 0.5%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise, and Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves.

For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Venture Capital: From Doriotte to Power Law
data.description:
Author: insights4vc Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The history of venture capital is essentially a history of the institutionalization of risk. This story did not begin in the modern Silicon-based offices of Sandhill Road, but rather in the industrial heartland of pre-war France and the solemn, disciplined classrooms of Harvard Business School. This industry evolved from Georges Doriot's paternalistic, institutionally-driven philosophy to the radical, data-driven investment paradigm of modern Silicon Valley's power law model—a transformation far more than just a change in financial strategy.

It reveals the profound evolution of human society in identifying talent, allocating capital for the unknown, and ultimately building the future. Using Spencer E. Ant's *Creative Capital* and Sebastian Malabi's *Power Law* as our core perspectives, we trace this development trajectory, revealing the shifting power dynamics between creative holders and the capital providers who realize those creative ideas.

The Cognitive Logic of Risk: The underlying logic of venture capital differs fundamentally from traditional finance. Most financial sectors, whether foreign exchange markets, bond trading, or public stock exchanges, operate within a safe framework of normal distribution. In these systems, observations revolve around the mean, and extreme tail events are statistically rare enough to be managed as outliers. Bond traders seek stable returns, and hedge fund managers consider doubling their portfolios within three years a remarkable achievement. Venture capital, however, is a "grand slam business," not a typical home run business. In this world, outliers not only affect the mean, but they are the mean themselves. Marabi points out that the power law is a universal principle: winners widen the gap at an accelerating, exponential rate. In a typical venture capital fund, a very small number of investments—perhaps only 5% of the allocated capital—contribute more than 60% of the total return. This mathematical reality dictates a unique cognitive system: most ideas are insignificant; only those seemingly impossible ideas possess true value. This demands a specific type of investor: one who can accept a 90% failure rate, as long as the remaining 10% can fundamentally change the human condition. The evolution of venture capital is a shift from a qualitative, mentorship-based model to a quantitative, mathematically-driven approach that embraces the unconventional. As Vinod Khosla famously said, progress depends on "those who don't play by the rules." This idea was fully embodied in 2010 when Stanford geneticist Patrick Brown presented Khosla with an "absurd" presentation proposing to completely replace the meat processing industry with plant-based heme. Khosla's initial response was "That's impossible!", followed by a $3 million check. This encapsulates the modern venture capitalist mindset: if an idea has a one percent chance of success and corresponds to a trillion-dollar market, then this risk is not only acceptable but must be taken.

Industrial Roots and the Birth of ARD

To understand the modern era of "sovereign founders," we must first trace back to the paternalistic origins of the industry, represented by Georges Doriot. In *Creative Capital*, Ant meticulously recounts that Doriot came from the French industrial elite. His father, Auguste Doriot, was a pioneer of Peugeot automobiles, involved in the manufacture of some of the earliest cars, and won the 1894 Paris-Rouen endurance race, a rigorous test of mechanical reliability.

Arthur Rock and the Fairchild Rebellion

While Doriot was building his empire in Boston, a more radical form of finance was emerging in the West. The key turning point, which Malabi calls “the liberation of capital,” occurred in the summer of 1957—the same year DEC invested—at the Shockley Semiconductor Laboratory in Mountain View, California. Nobel laureate and father of semiconductors, William Shockley, was also a “tyrannical despot,” subjecting employees to lie detector tests and belittling their intelligence.

Eight top PhD researchers under Shockley, including Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce, decided to resign en masse. In the era of "organizational men" in the 1950s, such a collective defection was unheard of, and loyalty was the highest virtue in a company. The "Traitorous Eight" did not initially want to start their own business, but simply hoped to be employed by a better-managed company as a team. Their letter eventually reached Arthur Locke, a young MBA at Haydenstone investment bank in New York.

... Locke realized that top-tier research talent, coupled with the rebellious spirit displayed by this act, presented an unprecedented business opportunity. He persuaded them to abandon employers and start their own businesses. He raised $1.4 million from Sherman Fairchild to found Fairchild Semiconductor. This was the birth of "adventure capital": liberating talent from traditional hierarchies and creating the organizational structure best suited for innovation. Fairchild became the core engine of the Silicon Valley miracle: by 2014, 70% of Silicon Valley's publicly traded companies could be traced back to this act of rebellion. In 1961, Locke partnered with Tommy Davis to establish Davis-Locke, becoming the first venture capital firm to adopt a private limited partnership structure, further refining this model. This structural shift tolled the death knell for the Dorriote publicly traded company model. The Triumph of the Limited Partnership: ARD's failure to become a mainstream venture capital template demonstrates that the history of venture capital is shaped not only by investor psychology but also by legal and financial structures. As a publicly traded investment firm, ARD was subject to the strict constraints of the Investment Company Act and close oversight by the SEC. This structure made experimentation cumbersome, compensation inflexible, and incentives weak. In an industry where a very few outliers can contribute the majority of returns, the inability to share substantial profits with investment professionals is not a minor design flaw but a fatal one. Private limited partnerships offer a superior structure. The new venture capital firms are no longer publicly traded companies with dispersed shareholdings, but rather private contractual arrangements between limited partners who provide capital and general partners who find projects, manage post-investment activities, and share profits. This simultaneously solves several problems: reducing regulatory hurdles, strengthening incentive consistency, and allowing venture capitalists to be rewarded through incidental income rather than fixed salaries. In fact, the limited partnership structure provides a more suitable structure for power-law businesses, ultimately aligning asset class returns with managerial compensation. However, the victory of the limited partnership structure was not purely a private sector achievement. A series of public policy decisions gradually transformed venture capital from a niche financing method into a scalable institutional asset class. The first important signal came from the Small Business Investment Act of 1958. The Small Business Administration (SBIC) pointed out that this act stemmed directly from a 1957 Federal Reserve study: small businesses lacked credit support and struggled to keep up with technological changes. To address this, the federal government launched the SBIC program, with the SBIC licensing, regulating, and funding privately managed investment firms to provide long-term debt and equity financing to high-risk small businesses. In other words, the federal government did not invent venture capital, but it explicitly acknowledged the structural financing gap and began building an institutional framework to address it. Subsequent tax policies improved the economics of entrepreneurship and investment risk. In the late 1970s and early 1980s, the top federal tax rate on long-term capital gains was significantly reduced: Treasury data shows that the rate was 49% before the 1978 reforms, dropped to 28% after the 1978 Revenue Act, and further decreased to 20% after the 1981 Economic Recovery Tax Act. These changes did not alone create the modern venture capital industry, but they significantly increased the after-tax attractiveness of high-growth equity investments. In areas where returns are highly concentrated in a few blockbuster exits, lower capital gains taxes reinforced the incentive to fund high-risk innovation rather than investing in safer income-generating assets. The truly decisive turning point came from pension capital. As Paul Gampers and Josh Lerner noted, between 1946 and 1977, the inflow of new venture capital funds into the United States never exceeded several hundred million dollars annually, typically falling far short. A key change occurred in 1979 when the Department of Labor reinterpreted the ERISA “prudent person” rule. Prior to this, many pension trustees avoided venture capital because investing in a single startup could be deemed imprudent. The 1979 clarification allowed prudence to be assessed from the perspective of the entire portfolio and its diversification, rather than a single risk position. This rule change opened the door for pension funds to become principal limited partners in venture capital partnerships. Its impact was immediate and transformative. Venture capital fundraising rose from approximately $450 million in 1979 to over $5 billion in 1983. This was not merely growth, but a paradigm shift. Once pension funds entered the asset class, venture capital was no longer a semi-amateur activity funded by wealthy families, industrial patrons, and scattered institutions. It has become a replicable financial industry, with sufficient scale to support specialized funds, larger rounds, longer holding periods, and more ambitious technology bets. The modern venture capital ecosystem—focused funds, formal limited partnerships—general partnerships, and institutionalized search for outliers—relies on both the talent of individual investors and this regulatory deregulation. If the Doriot era gave venture capital moral legitimacy, the limited partnership era gave it financial scalability. The core lesson is that venture capital's rise to dominance is not solely due to smarter investors, but rather because organizational forms, tax treatments, and public policies ultimately align with the economics of high-risk innovation. The government did not replace private judgment, but it decisively shaped the conditions under which private judgment could be exercised on a large scale. Therefore, the rise of venture capital should not be understood merely as a market story, but rather as a story of institutional design. Culture and Social Networks The geographical shift of venture capital focus from Boston to Silicon Valley in the 1980s stemmed from what Annalee Saxonnin called "regional advantage." Boston's Route 128 was already a high-tech hub before Silicon Valley was named, and in 1965, the number of tech professionals there was three times that of Silicon Valley. However, the two places had diametrically opposed cultures. Boston companies were led by Harvard-background leaders, emulating General Motors, emphasizing self-sufficiency and hierarchical systems. In contrast, Silicon Valley developed a decentralized industrial system, with companies outsourcing parts and freely exchanging information in places like Walker Wheel Bar. This "networked" culture was further reinforced by California law's non-enforcement of non-compete agreements. Venture capitalists were the main disseminators of this network. Professionals like Bill Young of Sutter Hill Ventures would invite the “smartest people” to lunch, asking them to recommend the next “best person,” systematically building a network of relationships to quickly activate when new opportunities arose. In the 1970s, the “active interventionist capital” model replaced Doriot’s “paternalistic” model. Represented by Don Valentine of Sequoia Capital and Tom Perkins of Kleiner Perkins, venture capitalists of this era were successful managers at companies like Fairchild and Hewlett-Packard. They didn't just provide funding; they imprinted their will on the post-investment companies. Tom Perkins is known for his "risk-stage" financing. At Tientronics, he brought in advisors to mitigate technological risks before making additional investments; at Genentech, the first major biotechnology company, he urged the founders to outsource research to existing labs to conserve cash—this deep intervention yielded a 200-fold return for Kleiner Perkins. Don Valentine was equally assertive with Atari, forcing the company to focus on the "home ping" market and subsequently pushing for its sale to Warner Communications. This is a "finance beyond finance" model: investors skip spreadsheets and focus on assessing character, resilience, and the "book value of knowledge." Arthur Locke would ignore financial forecasts, go straight to the founder's resume, ask open-ended questions, and observe in long silences whether the entrepreneur would break down under pressure. If the founder "lives in an ideal world rather than in reality," Locke would abandon the project. Youth Rebellion and Sovereign Founders The most recent evolution of venture capital began at the turn of the century, with power shifting significantly towards entrepreneurs. This "youth rebellion" was ignited by the success of Google and Facebook, companies that not only achieved scale but also complete market dominance. In earlier times, venture capitalists like Don Valentine had the right to fire founders; in the Cisco case, the founder was ultimately ousted due to a conflict with the professional CEO hired by Valentine. However, by 2005, abundant capital and the emergence of brand growth equity changed the game. After being "hurt" by Sequoia Capital in the Plaxo project, Sean Parker advised Mark Zuckerberg on how to maintain control of Facebook. Peter Thiel's Founders Fund elevated this shift to an ideology. Thiel believed that venture capital should no longer "polish" founders through mentorship, but should embrace "corporate monarchy," seeking out "rough gems" and allowing them to lead unchallenged. This era introduced dual-class share structures: founders retained super voting rights after the IPO. SoftBank's Masayoshi Son and DST Global's Yuri Milner's "kingmaker" capital accelerated this shift. In 2009, Milner invested $200 million in Facebook, valuing the company at $10 billion, without demanding a board seat—a deal Zuckerberg couldn't refuse. This "non-intervention" model indicates that for the most promising outliers, venture capitalists are no longer parents or bosses, but service providers. The shift towards a founder-centric, risk-driven model has created both the greatest successes and the most infamous failures of the digital age. The "blitzscaling" model—mass hiring without rigorous vetting and launching unfinished products to quickly seize market share—thrives in a deliberately lax, "founder-friendly" ecosystem. Malabi emphasizes that the obsession with "lone geniuses" fostered arrogance at WeWork and Uber. The radical culture of Uber founder Travis Kalanick ultimately evolved into a "Shakespearean" tragedy of ambition and self-absorption; Benchmark Capital's Bill Gurley was forced to launch a campaign to remove the founder he had personally supported. Similarly, WeWork's expansion, backed by SoftBank, demonstrates that massive amounts of venture capital can sustain unprofitable business models for too long, ultimately destroying economic value. These failures point to "board contagion": a lack of board oversight leads to damage to corporate reputations due to leaders' unbridled impulses. The Ethics and Geopolitics of the New Future As venture capital becomes a global force, its social impact faces increasing scrutiny. The industry remains a tightly knit circle with severely insufficient diversity: as of 2020, women comprised only 16% of investment partners, and Black partners only 3%. This demographic "social clustering" leads to a disproportionate flow of funds to founders with backgrounds similar to investors, systematically excluding a large pool of talent. However, the positive value of venture capital remains strong. Although critics argue that VCs only pursue success, academic research confirms that VC-backed companies contribute 89% of listed companies' R&D spending and have a much higher success rate for IPOs than non-VC-backed companies. Between 1995 and 2019, VC-backed companies accounted for 47% of non-financial IPOs in the United States. Furthermore, this model has become a pillar of national power. China's rise as a technological competitor stems directly from the introduction of the Silicon Valley VC model. Partnering institutions like Sequoia China applied power-law logic to the Chinese internet, creating a digital economy comparable to that of the United States. In a world shifting from tangible assets (factories and machines) to intangible assets (software and data), deeply involved VC models are increasingly becoming the only effective way to allocate capital. Conclusion The evolution of venture capital from George Doriot's "Inception" to the modern power-law era reveals a fundamental shift in the way humans innovate. Doriot imbued the industry with ethical and institutional legitimacy, proposing that funding "noble" ideas was a worthwhile endeavor in mature societies. Arthur Locke provided "liberated capital," freeing talent from the rigid hierarchies of the 1950s. Valentine and Perkins refined tools for active intervention, eliminating "hot risk" and building industry pillars. Today, the industry pushes outlier logic to mathematical extremes. Despite the significant governance challenges brought about by the shift towards founder-led monarchy and blitzkrieg expansion, it remains the most efficient crucible for technological discovery to date. As a "third institution" between the market and the firm, it combines the strategic vision of the firm with the ruthless price signaling discipline of the market. The evolution of venture capital demonstrates that the future cannot be predicted by analyzing past patterns; it must be discovered through high-risk experiments by those who "play by their own rules." By supporting the impossible, this special group of financial professionals has made the modern world possible. Just as Auguste Doriot's father once asked his son, who came in second, "Why not first?", venture capital has institutionalized this question within the global economy, refusing to accept anything less than a radical transformation of the human condition.

data.title: A Comprehensive Look at the Real Threat of Quantum Computers to Bitcoin (Part 2)
data.description:
Written by: Eli Nagar   Translated by: Block unicorn

After publishing Part 1, I wanted to see if people were interested and if it was worth exploring further. Reader feedback made this decision very easy.

So, Part 2 is here.

Part 5: Google's Big News (March 2026)

Note: This section contains more technical information.

If you find this difficult to understand, here's a brief summary: A significant research paper published by Google, Stanford University, and other institutions shows that breaking Bitcoin's encryption is 20 times easier than previously thought, and requires fewer than 500,000 quantum computing units, not millions. Future quantum computers could crack a Bitcoin private key in about 9 minutes, less than the approximately 10 minutes required for Bitcoin transaction confirmation. This means attackers could potentially steal a Bitcoin transaction before it's completed, or even during the transaction itself. Bitcoin's latest upgrade, Taproot (2021), actually makes things worse from a quantum perspective. It exposes crucial information, giving quantum attackers an unlimited amount of time to crack it. Approximately 6.9 million BTC (worth hundreds of billions of dollars) are stored in address types vulnerable to this type of attack. Google used a clever technique (zero-knowledge proof) to prove their calculations were correct, validating the results without revealing the actual attack blueprint. There isn't a quantum computer powerful enough for that yet. But they believe they're 20 times closer to the goal, and the Bitcoin community needs to start preparing now. Still not enough? Let's continue. While I was researching this topic, Google released a major announcement. On March 30, 2026, Google's quantum AI team, in collaboration with Stanford University (Dan Boneh), the Ethereum Foundation (Justin Drake), and the University of California, Berkeley, published a 57-page paper titled "Protecting Elliptic Curve Cryptocurrencies from Quantum Vulnerabilities: Resource Estimation and Mitigation Measures." I read the entire paper. This paper fundamentally changes my understanding of this threat (compared, of course, to previous assumptions). Why this paper is important: Previous estimates suggested that breaking Bitcoin's encryption would require millions of physical qubits. Google's paper demonstrates that it can be done with fewer than 500,000 qubits, a 20-fold reduction. This doesn't mean Bitcoin faces an imminent danger, but it does mean the danger is closer than the community previously assumed.

Resources Reduced by 20x

The paper proposes two optimized quantum circuit designs for running Shor's algorithm to solve the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP-256), a mathematical problem used to protect Bitcoin signatures:

TOFFOLI Gate

A basic quantum logic gate that operates on three qubits. It is a universal building block for quantum computing. The number of Tooffoli gates in the circuit is a key indicator of computational cost; fewer gates result in faster execution and less error accumulation.

For ease of understanding, here is a comparison with previous estimates: This reduction comes from algorithmic optimization, not hardware advancements. Researchers applied two key techniques: state reuse (deriving multiple private keys from the public key in a single execution by reusing the initial phase estimate state) and division batching (running multiple instances of the algorithm in parallel and merging modulo division operations; this technique is known as the "Montgomery trick"). These improvements are purely mathematical refinements to the implementation of Shor's algorithm.

9-Minute Attack

This discovery shocked me. On superconducting quantum architectures (such as "fast clock" machines like Google's Willow chip series), such an attack can be completed in approximately 9 minutes.

... The paper provides precise calculations: assuming the control system's reaction time is 10 microseconds (the standard for superconducting qubits) and each Toffoli gate has a 50% overhead, then on a superconducting CRQC, 70 million or 90 million Toffoli gates can be resolved in 18 or 23 minutes. However, the first half of the algorithm (universal to all addresses) can be pre-computed. Starting from this "pre-processed state," the attack takes approximately 9 or 12 minutes, depending on the circuit variant.

Dynamic Attack

In this type of attack, the quantum attacker waits for the victim to broadcast a transaction (thus exposing the public key), then races against time to derive the private key and submits a competing fraudulent transaction before the original transaction is confirmed. The 9-minute attack makes this attack effective against Bitcoin's approximately 10-minute block time.

Static Attack

This type of attack targets cryptocurrencies whose public keys are already publicly available on the blockchain (such as P2PK, P2TR, and reused addresses). The attacker has unlimited time and does not need to race against time. The attacker can steal these cryptocurrencies at any time.

Taproot: Worse Than We Thought

As a Bitcoin enthusiast, I was deeply saddened to read this article. One of the most important findings relates to Taproot (P2TR), Bitcoin's most recent major upgrade (2021). The paper explicitly states that P2TR has the same vulnerability level as the original P2PK format from 2009. The reason is that P2TR stores the modified public key directly in the locking script without using a hash value for hiding. This means that the public key is exposed on the blockchain from the moment the Bitcoin is received, allowing quantum attackers to attack ECDLP offline indefinitely. The paper cites BIP-360's P2MR (Pay-to-Merkle-Root) proposal as a potential solution, essentially removing the quantum-vulnerable key path expenditure from P2TR. P2MR retains many advantages of Taproot while avoiding the reintroduction of static attack vulnerabilities. The paper also calculates, using data from the bigquery-public-data.crypto_bitcoin database, that approximately 6.9 million Bitcoins across all protocol types are vulnerable. This figure is significantly higher than the previously cited 4 to 5 million bitcoins, primarily because Taproot's output is now also included in the vulnerability's scope. The paper identifies four different ways public keys can be exposed: Weak address vulnerabilities, where locking scripts directly leak the public key (P2PK, P2TR, P2MS). Address reuse vulnerabilities, where unlocking scripts in past spending transactions leak the public key. Public memory pool exposure, where the public key is visible during the transaction confirmation process. Off-chain exposure means public keys are shared elsewhere (exchanges, blockchain explorers, off-chain records). The paper makes a key distinction between two types of quantum computers, which have drastically different implications for when and how attacks become feasible: Scenario 2 may come first. The paper points out that slow-clock architectures may reach the required number of qubits earlier than fast-clock architectures because they may face fewer engineering challenges in scaling. If this is the case, static attacks will be easier to occur than dynamic attacks, thus providing the community with a brief window during which only the leaked keys are at risk. However, the paper warns that "the time interval between the breaking of a 32-bit ECDLP and the breaking of a 256-bit ECDLP could be very short," meaning that once a cracking begins, progress could be extremely rapid. The most thought-provoking part of the paper, which I found most compelling, is Section 8, which explores a highly politically sensitive issue: what happens to dormant assets—Bitcoins held by lost keys, deceased owners, or abandoned wallets? The paper estimates that approximately 2.3 million Bitcoins are stored in dormant P2PK addresses (unused for at least 5 years), which could be "harvested" by quantum computers. They use their simulations to show that a high-speed CRQC can accumulate approximately 2,000 Bitcoins from a single, large wallet and process hundreds of thousands of addresses within days to months. The paper outlines three community solutions under discussion: The paper also proposes a fourth solution: a "bad debt sidechain," a dedicated sidechain for recovery (similar to a "bad bank" in traditional finance). CRQC operators would send recovered dormant assets to this sidechain. This sidechain would implement relevant processes, identifying the true owner and returning assets through off-chain proof-of-ownership, while controlling the speed of asset redeployment to prevent market chaos. The paper also proposes a legal framework that draws on existing abandoned property laws (such as marine salvage and the revised U.S. Uniform Unclaimed Property Act). Under this framework, dormant tokens recovered through quantum recovery will be treated as "salvaged" property, and legal procedures will be used to identify the rightful owner and manage the proceeds, similar to how liquidators handle bankrupt assets.

Part Six: Proposed Solutions

Okay, so the threat is real. The next question I want to answer is: What measures are we taking to address it? The Bitcoin community has proposed several solutions, each involving a trade-off between security, practicality, and the difficulty of modifying Bitcoin's rules.

Post-Quantum Signature Schemes

Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

Cryptographic algorithms designed to withstand attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

Cryptographic algorithms designed to withstand attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

... Unlike existing algorithms (such as RSA and ECDSA), these algorithms are based on mathematical problems that quantum computers cannot solve efficiently. In August 2024, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) finalized its first PQC standard. Several PQC signature schemes could potentially replace ECDSA in Bitcoin: BIP-360: QuBit Soft Fork Significant changes (such as adding a new signature scheme) are called "soft forks." They tighten the rules, allowing older software to continue functioning while providing new functionality. This is a backward-compatible change to Bitcoin's rules. Older nodes will still accept all new blocks as valid; they simply won't understand the new features. This is less disruptive than a "hard fork" (which forces everyone to upgrade or split the network). BIP-360, proposed by Hunter Beast in mid-2024, is currently the most concrete proposal to add quantum resistance to Bitcoin. It introduces:

  • A new output type called P2QRH (Pay-to-Quantum-Resistant-Hash) as part of SegWit version 3

  • Algorithmic flexibility, a single version byte allows the network to support multiple PQC algorithms, so even if one algorithm fails, others can be used

  • Designed for soft forking for backward compatibility

Commit - Reveal Scheme

Therefore, QSB uses GPUs (powerful graphics cards) to search trillions of candidate results until a combination is found that allows Bitcoin's own verification system to accept the hash output as a valid signature. Security no longer relies on "key" mathematics that quantum computers can crack. Instead, it relies on the irreversibility of hash operations, which quantum computers cannot overcome.

How it works: Three stages

Spending a QSB transaction involves three steps.

Each step involves massive computation, essentially trying billions of combinations until the right one is found, much like a combination lock with trillions of possibilities: Pinning: First, the system searches for specific combinations of transaction parameters to lock the payment to a specific destination. Think of it as a tightly sealed envelope, one that even a quantum attacker cannot change the recipient's name without breaking the seal. This requires trying approximately 70 trillion combinations. Proving Ownership (Digest Round, x2): The transaction script contains 150 pre-submitted keys (hashable and therefore hidden). To prove ownership, the payer needs to reveal a specific subset of these keys, chosen by solving another hash puzzle. This is the true quantum-safe "signature": an attacker needs to guess the correct subset from trillions of possibilities, where a quantum computer has no shortcuts. For added security, this step is run twice. Integrating All Information (Assembly): Once the GPU cluster finds a valid solution to all three puzzles, the owner's secure device (such as a hardware wallet) assembles the final transaction and broadcasts it to the network. Importantly, the heavy GPU computation only involves public data, much like hiring workers to search for specific grains of sand on a beach. The real secret (your "key") never leaves your secure device. Even if the GPU cluster is hacked, the attacker cannot obtain any useful information. Security and Cost: The most important point about QSB security is that Shor's algorithm (the quantum attack that threatens Bitcoin) offers no advantage over it. The entire scheme is built on hash functions, which quantum computers cannot effectively crack. Even the Grover algorithm (another quantum technology that can partially speed up hash operations) is far more secure than any practically attackable level. Regarding cost: the GPU computation required to complete a QSB transaction costs approximately $75 to $150 (recommended configuration, renting cloud GPUs). A lighter configuration costs approximately $40 to $80. Computation can be distributed across any number of GPUs; the more GPUs, the faster the speed, similar to how Bitcoin mining works. Limitations QSB is only applicable to older Bitcoin transactions. It relies on an older feature called FindAndDelete, which was removed in Bitcoin's 2017 SegWit upgrade. It cannot secure Bitcoins with modern address formats (SegWit, Taproot), supporting only the older P2SH format. It cannot be sent over regular networks. Regular Bitcoin nodes will reject QSB transactions as non-standard transactions. They must be submitted directly to mining pools willing to accept them. It is costly and complex. Each transaction requires a GPU cost of $75 to $150. Additionally, you need to manage special keys on secure devices and coordinate with miners. This is far from a simple "scan and send" approach. Breaking Bitcoin's limitations. The recommended setup uses exactly 201 opcodes (the absolute maximum allowed by Bitcoin) and nearly reaches the 10,000-byte script limit. There's no room to add other features. It also doesn't support the Lightning Network (Bitcoin's Faster Payments layer). Lost tokens cannot be recovered. Like all other proposals, QSB requires users to actively transfer tokens. If you lose your keys (such as Satoshi Nakamoto's tokens), they remain vulnerable. Early stages. As of April 2026, the GPU search engine has been implemented and demonstrated, but end-to-end transaction assembly and on-chain broadcasting are not yet complete. Lessons I Learned from All This

The threat is real and closer than previously thought. A paper published by Google in March 2026 reduced the number of physical qubits required by a factor of 20, to below 500,000. Current hardware capacity is about 500 times less than expected, instead of the previously thought 10,000 times. Google estimates a 10% probability of a "Quantum Day" by 2032.

SHA-256 mining is secure. Grover's algorithm does not pose a real threat to Bitcoin's proof-of-work. The real danger lies in the signing process, not in the mining itself.

SHA-256 mining is secure. Grover's algorithm does not pose a real threat to Bitcoin's proof-of-work. The real danger lies in the signing process, not in the mining itself.

Approximately 6.9 million bitcoins (about 35% of the total supply) are at risk because their public keys have been compromised. This includes about 1.7 million bitcoins output by Satoshi Nakamoto via P2PK, and the ever-growing number of bitcoins in Taproot (P2TR) outputs. A Google paper states that Taproot (P2TR) has the same vulnerability level as P2PK. Taproot is a quantum-safe step backward. Similar to P2PK, P2TR exposes public keys on-chain and will account for 21.68% of all bitcoin transactions by 2025. On a fast-clocked CRQC, a 9-minute attack is feasible, meaning there is approximately a 41% probability that the private key can be derived within a single bitcoin block interval. This means that in-spending attacks against all transaction types will become a reality. BIP-360 is the most comprehensive long-term solution, but it requires community consensus and years of deployment. QSB is a remarkable technical achievement; it's a quantum-safe scheme operating under Bitcoin's existing rules. Due to its cost, complexity, and scripting limitations, it can only serve as an emergency escape plan, not a general-purpose solution. Now is the time to act. Google's paper explicitly urges all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities to "immediately join the migration to PQC." Bitcoin governance is slow; Taproot took approximately four years from proposal to activation. Quantum-resistant upgrades may take even longer, and the window of opportunity may be shorter than anticipated. When I started this research, I initially thought I would conclude, "It's okay, we still have decades." But I can't say that now. As a Bitcoin enthusiast, I believe the community needs to take this issue seriously, not with panic, but with the same clear-headed and pragmatic approach that Bitcoin has taken to address all challenges to date. If this article has helped you better understand the quantum threat, please share it. The more Bitcoin enthusiasts truly understand this issue, the greater our chances of solving it together.
